questions.question: What is a force?
questions.correct_answer: A push or pull acting on an object
questions.explanation: Force occurs when two objects interact with each other and can change the motion or shape of an object.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in newtons and named after the scientist Isaac Newton.

questions.question: What are the effects of force?
questions.correct_answer: It can change speed, direction, shape, or start/stop motion of an object
questions.explanation: For example, kicking a football changes its speed and direction.

questions.question: What are contact forces?
questions.correct_answer: Forces that act only when objects touch each other
questions.explanation: Examples include muscular force and friction.

questions.question: What are non-contact forces?
questions.correct_answer: Forces that act without physical contact
questions.explanation: Examples include magnetic force, gravitational force, and electrostatic force.

questions.question: What is friction?
questions.correct_answer: A force that opposes the motion of an object
questions.explanation: Friction acts between surfaces in contact and slows down moving objects.

questions.question: What is gravitational force?
questions.correct_answer: The force with which the Earth attracts objects towards itself
questions.explanation: This force causes objects to fall to the ground.

questions.question: What is weight?
questions.correct_answer: The force with which Earth pulls an object
questions.explanation: Weight is a force and is measured in newtons.

questions.question: What is buoyant force?
questions.correct_answer: An upward force exerted by a liquid on an object placed in it
questions.explanation: This force helps some objects float in water.

questions.question: Why do some objects float while others sink?
questions.correct_answer: It depends on the balance between gravitational force and buoyant force
questions.explanation: If buoyant force equals or exceeds weight, the object floats; otherwise it sinks.

notes.title: Force
notes.content: A force is a push or pull acting on an object due to interaction with another object.

notes.title: Effects of Force
notes.content: Force can change the speed, direction, shape, or motion of an object.

notes.title: Contact Forces
notes.content: Contact forces require physical contact between objects, such as muscular force and friction.

notes.title: Non-contact Forces
notes.content: Non-contact forces act from a distance, such as gravitational, magnetic, and electrostatic forces.

notes.title: Buoyancy
notes.content: Buoyancy is the upward force exerted by a fluid that allows objects to float.

facts.fact: Friction always acts opposite to the direction of motion.
The weight of an object can change depending on the gravitational force.
Mass of an object remains constant everywhere, but weight can change.
